design_philosophy.core_loop: Protect neighborhood houses from burglars by traveling via sled to threatened locations, then engaging in platformer combat using crafted weapons to fill a damage meter before the robbers complete their theft
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (ammunition, weapon durability) and time pressure (20-40 minute timer before police arrive, per-house robbery progress meter)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as Kevin McCallister defending his neighborhood through improvised weaponry and tactical house defense
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Unusually hybrid design mixing top-down sled navigation with side-scrolling platformer combat; emphasis on weapon crafting from found items rather than pre-built arsenals; multi-house territory defense rather than single-location protection

difficulty.continues_system: Game over occurs if all neighborhood houses become 'flooded' (robbers complete theft before damage meter fills)
difficulty.checkpointing: Per-house checkpointing; houses locked permanently once flooded but other houses remain accessible
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player selects difficulty affecting timer duration (20 minutes normal, 40 minutes hard); difficulty impacts police arrival speed and thus window for defense

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon crafting system: combine found items (glue, snow, light bulbs, sound waves, coals, etc.) with gun types (rifle, bazooka, launcher, mortar) to create weapons with varying ballistics and effects; ammunition refills upon completing certain weapon configurations
progression.unlock_mechanism: Items scattered throughout neighborhood houses unlock new weapon combinations; higher difficulty tiers unlock extended time window
replayability_hooks: Multiple houses to defend creates different encounter sequences
Weapon crafting combinations provide strategic variety
Difficulty tiers alter time pressure and game length

world_building_techniques: Multi-house neighborhood setting from film source material
Wet Bandits' signature 'flooding' calling card creates environmental narrative of theft progression
Ammunition/weapon types themed around household items (light bulbs, coals) reinforce home-defense fantasy

boss_design.philosophy: Burglars (Harry and Marv) function as persistent threats rather than discrete boss encounters; they serve as roving antagonists that appear in houses rather than as isolated confrontations
notable_bosses: Harry and Marv (Wet Bandits) - appear progressively across neighborhood houses with escalating difficulty

danger_signals: House robbery meter filling (indicates active theft in progress)
environmental_storytelling: Water damage left in robbed houses serves as visual marker of Bandits' presence and progress
Neighborhood layout reveals available defensive positions and item locations
Sled travel mechanic creates sense of patrolling territory under threat
